activities_projects.title: Sociolinguistic Analysis: The Concept of 'Face'
activities_projects.objective: Analyze how language is used to maintain social dignity and 'save face'
steps: Research the linguistic concept of 'Politeness Theory' (Brown and Levinson).
Identify three instances in the text where characters use 'indirectness' to avoid embarrassing someone.
Write a 150-word analysis on how the 'right thing' often involves protecting the 'face' of the listener.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Students understand the psychological and sociological foundations of communication
activities_projects.evaluation: Critical depth, use of linguistic terminology, and textual evidence (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Deconstructing Sarcasm vs. Wit
activities_projects.objective: Distinguish between harmful sarcasm and constructive wit
steps: Find a line in the story that could be interpreted as sarcastic if said in a different tone.
Analyze the 'Intention' (Illocutionary force) of the speaker in that moment.
Write a 100-word essay arguing why the 'Right Thing' sometimes requires witty truth-telling rather than just polite agreement.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Students develop an advanced understanding of pragmatics and intent in speech
activities_projects.evaluation: Logical consistency and depth of distinction (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The Diplomacy of Conflict Resolution
activities_projects.objective: Apply the chapter’s lessons to complex diplomatic or professional scenarios
steps: Imagine a high-stakes scenario (e.g., a disagreement between two world leaders or business partners).
Using the techniques from the chapter, draft a 200-word 'Diplomatic Brief' that resolves the conflict without making either side look bad.
Incorporate at least three 'power phrases' found in the text.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Students practice professional writing and strategic communication
activities_projects.evaluation: Persuasiveness, tone, and strategic use of language (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Linguistic Archiving: The Etymology of Courtesy
activities_projects.objective: Explore the historical origins of formal social language
steps: Select 4 formal words from the text: *Courtesy*, *Etiquette*, *Sincere*, and *Diplomatic*.
Research their Latin, French, or Greek roots and how their meanings evolved from physical actions to social concepts.
Explain how the historical meaning of 'Etiquette' (a ticket or label) relates to 'saying the right thing' today.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Students enhance vocabulary through historical and cultural linguistics
activities_projects.evaluation: Accuracy of research and depth of connection (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Satirical Writing: The 'Perfectly Wrong' Thing
activities_projects.objective: Use satire to highlight the absurdity of social pressures
steps: Write a satirical 3-stanza poem about a character who is so obsessed with 'Saying the Right Thing' that they never say anything at all.
Use a sophisticated rhyme scheme (e.g., ABAB) and high-tier vocabulary.
Focus on the 'Wit and Humour' aspect by mocking the fear of making a social mistake.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Students practice stylistic writing and social commentary
activities_projects.evaluation: Creative voice, technical poetic structure, and satirical depth (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Syntactic Analysis: The Power of the Passive Voice
activities_projects.objective: Analyze how passive voice is used to deflect blame politely
steps: Identify 3 sentences in the text where a character avoids saying 'You did this' (Active) and says 'This happened' (Passive).
Explain why the passive voice is often the 'right' choice in a conflict.
Rewrite a paragraph of direct accusations into a 'polite' paragraph using passive and conditional structures.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Students master advanced syntax for nuanced social communication
activities_projects.evaluation: Grammatical accuracy and understanding of syntactic choice (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The 'Social Rescue' Script: Cultural Context
activities_projects.objective: Translate a 'social rescue' scenario across different cultural norms
steps: Take a core scene from the chapter where a character makes a social error.
Rewrite the scene for two different cultural settings (e.g., a formal Japanese tea ceremony vs. a casual Australian barbecue).
Explain how the 'Right Thing' changes in each version despite the mistake being the same.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Students develop intercultural competence and adaptive writing skills
activities_projects.evaluation: Cultural sensitivity and creative adaptation (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Critical Reflection: The Burden of Sincerity
activities_projects.objective: Debate the morality of 'social lies' vs. 'brutal honesty'
steps: Analyze a moment in the story where being 'polite' requires a character to hide their true feelings.
Write a 120-word reflective summary: Is it always 'right' to prioritize the listener's feelings over the truth?
Define the term 'White Lie' in the context of the 'Wit and Humour' unit.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Students engage in ethical inquiry and thematic synthesis
activities_projects.evaluation: Clarity of argument and maturity of reflection (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Annotation: The Hidden Subtext
activities_projects.objective: Deconstruct a complex dialogue to find the unsaid meaning
materials: Photocopy of a page from the text
steps: Highlight the 'Surface Text' (what is said) in one color and the 'Subtext' (what is actually meant) in another.
Write footnotes for 5 lines of dialogue explaining the social 'strategy' the speaker is using.
Focus on the use of pauses and emphasis (italics) in the text.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Students master the deconstruction of sophisticated dialogue
activities_projects.evaluation: Insightfulness of annotations and understanding of subtext (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Presentation: The 'Wit as a Weapon' Seminar
activities_projects.objective: Synthesize the entire unit’s theme through a formal presentation
steps: Prepare a 5-minute presentation arguing that 'Wit' is the ultimate social survival tool.
Use 'Say the Right Thing' and 'Dr. Dolittle' as your two primary sources.
Conclude with a 'Guide to Witty Etiquette' for the 21st century.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Students sharpen public speaking and intertextual synthesis skills
activities_projects.evaluation: Rhetorical skill, use of evidence, and visual clarity (10 marks)
